brand_understanding.what_they_do: First-aid, health and child-safety education for parents of little humans, founded by an Emergency Clinical Nurse Educator.
brand_understanding.who_for_v2_audience: Megan herself — this version is built so she can SEE what Season 1 contains, navigate her own content, and feel inspired about what to do with it next.
brand_understanding.voice: Warm, direct, practical, gently expert, anti-jargon, slightly playful, scrapbooky.
brand_understanding.north_star: Megan opens this and instantly sees the gold buried in each episode — every guest's lane, every line that landed, every question worth playing again.
brand_understanding.huddle_promise: 5 experts. 5 lanes. Real questions Megan asked. The lines that landed.

season.title: The Huddle — Season 1
season.tagline: Five experts. Five lanes. The gold from every conversation.

host.name: Megan Harvey
host.role: Host · Founder · Emergency Clinical Nurse Educator · Mum of two
host.bio_short: Megan spent 10+ years teaching families face-to-face in Melbourne hospitals. The Huddle is the on-demand version of that conversation — where she sits down with the experts she trusts and asks the things parents actually want to know.

season.intro_promise: Welcome to The Huddle, the place where parents come together to learn, laugh, and feel a little less alone in this wild ride called parenting. I'm Megan from Help at Hand Education.
season.outro_promise: Hey, thanks for joining us. I hope you got something out of today and learnt a lot. If you love this chat, don't forget to tell your friends and family about The Huddle by Help at Hand Education. We'll see you next time.

topics.label: Body & recovery
topics.blurb: Pelvic floor, exercise in pregnancy, support wear, abdominal separation, postnatal.

topics.label: Sleep & self-settling
topics.blurb: Routines, the 4-month regression, sleeping through, gentle settling.

topics.label: Mental load & PND
topics.blurb: Postnatal depression, fill-your-cup, sharing the load with a partner.

topics.label: Kids' teeth
topics.blurb: First dentist visit, fluoride, thumb sucking, knocked-out teeth, brushing battles.

topics.label: Feeding little humans
topics.blurb: Solids, fussy phase, choking vs gagging, neurodiverse eating, allergens.

topics.label: Speech & communication
topics.blurb: Late talkers, milestones, stuttering, the OWL strategy, social communication.

topics.label: First aid & 000 moments
topics.blurb: When to call 000, choking, knocked-out teeth, the moments that matter most.

guest.name: Claire Maule
guest.bio_paragraph: Claire is a women's health physio of 17 years — she landed in the field on her last clinical placement and never left. Mum of three. Works on the maternity ward at Epworth Freemasons and runs Bloom Physio for telehealth.
guest.rawness_note: Honest about muddling through her first pregnancy despite knowing the science — felt tired, sick, juggling. Got better pregnancy by pregnancy. Real about the 'I lived on toasties with my first' parent reality.

journey.opening_worry: My maternal health nurse banged on about pelvic floor. Post-it notes on the car at every red light. Why?
journey.the_turn: It's a hammock. It sags. Squeeze AND let it go. And the support wear that actually changes things — medical-grade, not activewear.
journey.what_landed: Fill your cup up first — you're a better parent for it.

questions.question_asked: What's your background — how did you get into women's physiotherapy?
questions.answer_excerpt: Did her ACL in netball at 17. Loved the rehab. Last clinical placement was at a women's health practice in East Melbourne — never left.

questions.question_asked: What did you notice once you became pregnant yourself?
questions.answer_excerpt: Did 10 years of women's-health physio before her own kids. 'Muddled through' the first pregnancy despite knowing it all. Got better with each one.

questions.question_asked: What are the exercise recommendations through pregnancy?
questions.answer_excerpt: Most days, ideally all. 30–60 min. Cardio + strength + core + stretching. Walking, swimming, Pilates, yoga. Cuts gestational diabetes and preeclampsia risk.

questions.question_asked: Why is the pelvic floor so important — even before babies?
questions.answer_excerpt: It's a sling — a hammock at the base of the pelvis. Pregnancy hormones plus the weight of the baby make it sag. Quality matters more than quantity — squeeze AND let go.

questions.question_asked: What's the main concern pregnant women come to see you about?
questions.answer_excerpt: Bladder leakage with cough/sneeze/laugh. Urgency. Vaginal heaviness — often mistaken for prolapse. Pelvic girdle pain masking as low-back pain.

questions.question_asked: What support wear should you actually buy — is more expensive better?
questions.answer_excerpt: Two categories: medical-grade compression vs activewear-grade. If pregnancy is comfortable, activewear is fine. If there's pain or vulval varicosities, medical-grade is worth the money.

questions.question_asked: When should you book in to see a women's-health physio?
questions.answer_excerpt: Three appointments ideally. Around 12–13 weeks for screening. Mid-pregnancy when aches start. End-of-pregnancy for perineal massage and birth prep.

questions.question_asked: What about abdominal separation — should everyone get checked?
questions.answer_excerpt: Don't worry about the measurement during pregnancy — all tummy muscles stretch. Most hospitals have a physio that comes around. Otherwise check at 4–6 weeks postnatal.

questions.question_asked: As a parent yourself, what's your biggest piece of advice for new parents?
questions.answer_excerpt: Two things. If you've got a partner, work as a team — sit down weekly and divide it. And fill your cup up. If you fill your cup up, you're a better parent.

quotes.text: I didn't realize that bladder leakage wasn't normal, or I've actually got a little bit of pelvic heaviness.
quotes.attribution: Claire Maule · what mums often realise too late

quotes.text: If you fill your cup up first, you're a better parent.
The hammock starts to sag a little bit as your pregnancy progresses. So if the muscle isn't strong, that's when bladder leakage can happen.
quotes.attribution: Claire Maule · on the pelvic floor

guest.name: Bianca Burge
guest.bio_paragraph: Bianca's a midwife of 13 years turned sleep consultant. Plan B was born out of her own two boys being shocking sleepers and the realisation that she could help families through what she'd been through. Mum to Oscar (8) and Hugo (6).
guest.rawness_note: Opens up about her postnatal depression — the 'overwhelming darkness in the evenings' after Oscar's birth, breastfeeding PTSD, recognising the signs faster the second time. Lost her own mum at 17. The candour is the gift of this episode.

journey.opening_worry: Instagram is full of noise about how my baby 'should' sleep. What's actually true?
journey.the_turn: Babies cry to communicate. Sleep training can be as gentle as you need. And the truth no one talks about — postnatal depression is real, survivable, and not a failure.
journey.what_landed: It's actually more detrimental for them not to sleep properly than to short-term sleep train.

questions.question_asked: How did you get into midwifery and sleep consulting?
questions.answer_excerpt: 13 years a midwife. Plan B started after her boys (Oscar 8, Hugo 6) turned out to be shocking sleepers — she realised she could help.

questions.question_asked: How do you block out the social-media noise about sleep?
questions.answer_excerpt: First 3 months babies are still in the fourth trimester. Don't put pressure on yourself. Influencers set unrealistic expectations.

questions.question_asked: Are routines essential? When should you start?
questions.answer_excerpt: From around 4 months. The 4-month regression is babies developing adult-like sleep cycles. You'll see the wheels go creaky around 6–12 weeks when melatonin kicks in.

questions.question_asked: What actually IS 'sleeping through the night'?
questions.answer_excerpt: 6:30 to 6:30 with no feed in between. Newborns can't be expected to. Adults wake every 2–4 hours too — we just self-settle without realising.

questions.question_asked: Can you share your postnatal depression journey?
questions.answer_excerpt: Always had some anxiety. Lost her mum at 17. 'This isn't normal, this isn't baby blues, this is something else.' Got it again with Hugo, recognised the signs faster.

questions.question_asked: What are the biggest sleep-training misconceptions?
questions.answer_excerpt: That you leave your baby crying with no response. Sleep training can be as gentle as you need. It's nothing like the taboo it's been painted as.

questions.question_asked: Final advice for new parents?
questions.answer_excerpt: Don't put so much pressure on yourself. Enjoy the cuddles and newborn snuggles. The grass is always greener.

quotes.text: It's actually more detrimental to a baby's development for them not to sleep properly than what it is to short-term sleep train.
quotes.attribution: Bianca Burge · the line that settles families

quotes.text: I had this overwhelming kind of darkness that kind of came over me always in the evenings.
Whatever works for you at the end of the day. As long as they're healthy and you're loving them, it really doesn't matter.

guest.name: Isabelle Sayers
guest.bio_paragraph: Belle was meant to do commerce-law. A friend pointed her at the Bachelor of Oral Health at Melbourne Uni and she never looked back. 12 years in, mum of Charlotte and Harry. Charlotte loved having teeth brushed; Harry hated it. So Belle's been on both ends as a parent.
guest.rawness_note: Honest about the screaming-toothbrush phase she lived through with Harry — gives parents permission to keep going even when their kid hates it. Now Harry pulls out lengths of floss before she even asks.

journey.opening_worry: I think I was a bit late on first dentist visit. And the wriggly toothbrush battle is killing me.
journey.the_turn: First visit at age 1. Cavities are real even in baby teeth. Position matters more than struggle. And the punchy verdict if life gets in the way.
journey.what_landed: If you can only do one, do it at night.

questions.question_asked: When's the best time for the first dentist visit?
questions.answer_excerpt: Age 1. By then they have all the incisors and possibly first molars. We're also checking soft tissues. Even without all the teeth we want to see them.

questions.question_asked: Can kids really get cavities and decay?
questions.answer_excerpt: Yes — the stats are staggering. Many parents don't think it can happen to baby teeth. Important to understand the why so you can prevent it.

questions.question_asked: Is natural toothpaste safe for kids?
questions.answer_excerpt: Natural toothpastes don't contain fluoride. Fluoride is a proven protective force. Without it there isn't much point. Use a fluoridated, age-appropriate toothpaste.

questions.question_asked: Thumb sucking — when should you address it?
questions.answer_excerpt: Stop the behaviour by age 4 — first adult teeth come at 5–6. Tips: gentle reminders, distractions, yucky-tasting nail polish (paint all fingers!), socks at bedtime.

questions.question_asked: What if a child knocks a tooth out?
questions.answer_excerpt: Baby tooth — leave it out. Adult tooth — only touch the crown, rinse if dirty, pop it back in if you can. If not, keep it moist in saliva, water or milk. Time is critical.

questions.question_asked: Strategies for parents whose kids resist brushing?
questions.answer_excerpt: Introduce something in their mouth as soon as teeth appear — wet cloth, toothbrush. Position matters: cradle for babies, child-on-toilet hugged into you for older kids.

questions.question_asked: If you can only brush once a day, when should it be?
questions.answer_excerpt: Twice a day is hard. If you can only do one — do it at night. Asleep we don't produce as much saliva, and saliva protects teeth.

questions.question_asked: Final advice for parents in the thick of it?
questions.answer_excerpt: Find the people you trust and listen to ONE — not all of them. There's too much noise. Once you find your people it makes it so much easier.

quotes.text: If you can only do one, do it at night.
I know what to do dental-wise. But you know your child.
quotes.attribution: Belle Sayers · on parents trusting themselves

quotes.text: Find the people that I trusted, and then I just listened to one person.

guest.name: Olivia McArthur
guest.bio_paragraph: Olivia's a paediatric dietitian with 12 years' experience, specialising in fussy eating and the neurodiversity space — and she's a mum of FIVE. Got into nutrition through rowing in high school after an early brush with disordered eating. Started her private practice in 2018.
guest.rawness_note: Honest about the disordered eating in her family growing up and how it shaped her work. Also raw about the gagging-not-choking moment with her firstborn — out of the high chair, crying, baby smiling.

journey.opening_worry: I struggle big time with mealtime, and you have FIVE — how does that work at your dinner table?
journey.the_turn: 32 steps to eating. Posture before food. Choking is silent, gagging is loud. There's no rule that breakfast must be cereal — maximise their intake when their appetite is best.
journey.what_landed: It actually takes a typically developing child 15 to 20 times of trying a new food to actually accept it.

questions.question_asked: What's the fussy phase — and when does it hit?
questions.answer_excerpt: 12–24 months is brutal. Huge developmental growth, asserting independence, and you're teaching them to eat. Foods they ate get rejected. Back yourself — it's a phase.

questions.question_asked: When and how should you introduce solids?
questions.answer_excerpt: Around 6 months — not before 4. Look for head control, the tongue-extrusion reflex receding, leaning forward. Milk is still the most important nutrition until 8 months.

questions.question_asked: Why does posture in the high chair matter so much?
questions.answer_excerpt: 90-90-90 position. Feet grounded. If they're slouching they're using core to stay upright — focus comes off the food. Stack books or blocks if there's no foot support.

questions.question_asked: What's the difference between choking and gagging?
questions.answer_excerpt: Choking is silent — they could go purpley-blue, straight out of the chair. Gagging is loud, face goes red, often follows with a vomit. Gagging is normal and necessary.

questions.question_asked: How do you work with neurodivergent kids and food?
questions.answer_excerpt: Sensory profiles change everything. Super smellers, super tasters. ADHD meds impact appetite. Often kids eat well for 12–15 months then drop foods as their sensory profile kicks in.

questions.question_asked: Should kids really have to eat 'breakfast food' at breakfast?
questions.answer_excerpt: There's no rule. If you struggle to get a dinner meal in, offer it at breakfast — that's when they're most alert. Try going from kindy straight to early dinner.

questions.question_asked: What are the biggest misconceptions about feeding kids?
questions.answer_excerpt: Iron jumps from 0.7mg to 11mg at 6 months. Allergens (cow's milk, eggs, fish, nuts, soy) should be introduced early and often — twice a week to maintain exposure.

quotes.text: It actually takes a typically developing child 15 to 20 times of trying a new food to actually accept it.
quotes.attribution: Olivia McArthur · the rule of thumb

quotes.text: Choking is silent. Gagging is loud — face goes red, often follows with a vomit.
quotes.attribution: Olivia McArthur · the line that saves panic

quotes.text: There are actually 32 steps to eating, from looking at a food to swallowing it.
quotes.attribution: Olivia McArthur · why solids take patience

guest.name: Felicity Conlan
guest.bio_paragraph: Felicity wanted to work with kids and languages. A friend's brain aneurysm pulled her into speech pathology — watching that friend rebuild speech after losing it shaped everything. Master of Speech Pathology at La Trobe. Author of 'The Light Was Always There', a kids' book about hope written during the pandemic.
guest.rawness_note: Notes how often parents come into her office and the session becomes a 'psychology session' about their own speech challenges as kids — because back in the day this wasn't identified or diagnosed.

journey.opening_worry: How do parents know when to actually book a speech pathologist?
journey.the_turn: Communication breakdown is the flag — not just stutters and lisps. Speech, language, fluency, voice, pragmatics, literacy, and swallowing. The OWL strategy at home. The iPad doesn't talk back.
journey.what_landed: Make sure it's not you correcting your child all the time. Model — they say 'I go down the street', you say 'yes, you went down the street.'

questions.question_asked: How do parents know when to book a speech pathologist?
questions.answer_excerpt: Communication breakdown is the flag. Speech paths cover speech, expressive + receptive language, fluency, voice, pragmatics (social), literacy, and swallowing.

questions.question_asked: What's the most common concern parents bring you?
questions.answer_excerpt: 'I think my child doesn't have enough words' — late-talker referrals. And 'I'm not sure if they should be saying this sound — what are the milestones?'

questions.question_asked: What age should parents look at speech-sound milestones?
questions.answer_excerpt: Age 3 for sound milestones. F-for-TH ('thank' → 'fank') is the LAST sound to develop — don't intervene before age 4. Stuttering has a strong genetic link — see early.

questions.question_asked: What are the biggest misconceptions in your field?
questions.answer_excerpt: Stuttering on TV looks one way — there are many ways to stutter. People Google US sites without realising the Australian accent is different. Use Speech Pathology Australia.

questions.question_asked: What strategies can parents try at home?
questions.answer_excerpt: Interaction is everything. The iPad doesn't talk back. Use OWL: Observe, Wait, Listen. If they're playing with a train, talk about the train. Follow their lead.

questions.question_asked: Final advice for parents starting to navigate all this?
questions.answer_excerpt: Communication should be FUN. Don't correct your child constantly — model. They say 'I go down the street', you say 'yes, you went down the street'. Read books, sing songs.

quotes.text: Communication breakdown — that's actually what we look at. Not just stutters and lisps.
quotes.attribution: Felicity Conlan · what speech paths really do

quotes.text: Observe, wait, and listen. The iPad doesn't talk back — interaction is the key to speech and language development.
Make sure it's not you correcting your child all the time. Model — they say 'I go down the street', you say 'yes, you went down the street'.
